# Component Documentation Comprehensive guide to all React components in the application. ## Form Components (`src/components/form/`) ### AuButton **Location:** `src/components/form/button/AuButton.tsx` **Purpose:** Flexible button component with multiple variants and sizes. **Props:** ```typescript type PropsT = { areaPressed?: boolean; className?: string; children: React.ReactNode; form?: string; // Form ID for submit buttons kind?: AuButtonKindE; // Button variant onClick?: () => void; size?: AuButtonSizeE; // Button size testId?: string; type?: 'submit' | 'reset' | 'button'; disabled?: boolean; }; ``` **Key Features:** - Six button variants via `AuButtonKindE`: PRIMARY, SECONDARY, LINK, ICON, CHIPS, TRANSPARENT - Three sizes via `AuButtonSizeE`: SMALL, MEDIUM, LARGE - Prevents default on click (calls `e.preventDefault()`) - Accessible with aria-pressed support - Default type is 'button' (prevents accidental form submission) **Usage Example:** ```typescript import AuButton from 'Components/form/button/AuButton'; import { AuButtonKindE } from 'Components/form/button/AuButtonKindE'; import { AuButtonSizeE } from 'Components/form/button/AuButtonSizeE'; handleSubmit()} > Save Changes ``` **Dependencies:** `classnames` --- ### AuField **Location:** `src/components/form/field/AuField.tsx` **Purpose:** Form field wrapper that integrates with react-hook-form, providing label, input, validation, and error display. **Props:** ```typescript type MyFieldProps = { children: React.ReactElement; // Input element to wrap className?: string; clearValue?: AuFieldClearValueT; // Value for clear button defaultFocus?: boolean; // Auto-focus on mount htmlFor?: string; maxLength?: number; minLength?: number; name: string; // Required - field name label?: string; type?: string; placeholder?: string; pattern?: ValidationPatternT; // Regex validation replace?: ReplacePatternT; // Replace pattern on input required?: boolean; withoutValidation?: boolean; // Skip validation }; ``` **Key Features:** - Integrates with react-hook-form via `useController` - Built-in validation: required, minLength, maxLength, pattern - Optional clear button when value differs from `clearValue` - Real-time pattern replacement on input - Blocks invalid input based on pattern - Auto-focus support - Error display below input - Warns in dev mode if child props are overridden **Usage Example:** ```typescript import AuField from 'Components/form/field/AuField'; import { useFormContext } from 'react-hook-form'; // Inside a FormProvider ``` **Dependencies:** `react-hook-form`, `classnames`, `AuButton`, `ClearSvg` --- ### AuDropdown **Location:** `src/components/form/dropdown/AuDropdown.tsx` **Purpose:** Dropdown menu component for displaying action menus. **Usage:** Typically used for action menus in table rows or cards. **Dependencies:** Position-based dropdown with click-outside detection --- ### AuSelect **Location:** `src/components/form/select/AuSelect.tsx` **Purpose:** Select dropdown for choosing from options. **Props:** Accepts options as `SelectOptionT[]` type. **Usage:** Form select with react-hook-form integration. --- ### AuCheckbox **Location:** `src/components/form/checkbox/AuCheckbox.tsx` **Purpose:** Checkbox input component. **Usage:** Integrated with react-hook-form for boolean values. --- ### AuRadioGroup **Location:** `src/components/form/radioGroup/AuRadioGroup.tsx` **Purpose:** Radio button group component. **Usage:** Multiple choice selection with react-hook-form. --- ### AuDatePicker **Location:** `src/components/form/datePicker/AuDatePicker.tsx` **Purpose:** Date picker component. **Usage:** Date input with calendar UI. --- ### AuForm **Location:** `src/components/form/AuForm.tsx` **Purpose:** Form container that provides react-hook-form context. **Usage:** Wraps form fields with FormProvider. --- ### AuFormBlock **Location:** `src/components/form/formBlock/AuFormBlock.tsx` **Purpose:** Form section wrapper for grouping related fields. **Usage:** Visual grouping of form fields. --- ## Layout Components (`src/components/layouts/`) ### MainLayout **Location:** `src/components/layouts/main/MainLayout.tsx` **Purpose:** Main application layout with navigation menu, content area, footer, and AI assistant. **Props:** ```typescript type PropsT = { className?: string; children?: ReactNode; // Page content }; ``` **Key Features:** - Wraps content in `BaseLayout` - Includes `Menu` component in nav - Includes footer with logo - Includes `AIAssistant` component - Applies custom className to main content area **Structure:** ``` BaseLayout ├──
Menu ├──
{children} ├──
Logo └── AIAssistant ``` **Usage Example:** ```typescript import MainLayout from 'Components/layouts/main/MainLayout'; const MyPage = () => (

Page Content

 ); ``` **Dependencies:** `BaseLayout`, `Menu`, `AIAssistant` --- ### BaseLayout **Location:** `src/components/layouts/base/BaseLayout.tsx` **Purpose:** Base layout wrapper that provides fundamental structure. **Usage:** Usually not used directly - consumed by `MainLayout`. --- ### Menu **Location:** `src/components/layouts/main/menu/Menu.tsx` **Purpose:** Navigation menu component. **Key Features:** - Contains `Navigation` links - Includes `LogoutButton` --- ### Navigation **Location:** `src/components/layouts/main/menu/navigation/Navigation.tsx` **Purpose:** Navigation links for main pages. **Features:** Links to Transactions, Budgets, Bank Accounts, Categories, Profile --- ### LogoutButton **Location:** `src/components/layouts/main/menu/logoutButton/LogoutButton.tsx` **Purpose:** Logout button in menu. **Features:** Calls auth service to log out user --- ## Modal System (`src/components/modal/`) ### Modal **Location:** `src/components/modal/Modal.tsx` **Purpose:** Modal dialog component that uses HTML `` element and global state management. **Props:** ```typescript type PropsT = { children: ReactNode; // Modal content name: ModalNameE; // Modal identifier title: string; // Modal title type?: ModalTypeE; // Modal variant (REGULAR) }; ``` **Key Features:** - Uses native `` element with `showModal()` - Subscribes to global modals state via `useGlobalValue` - Automatically closes on unmount - Closes on outside click (via `useOutsideClickListener`) - Manages open/close based on presence in global state - Uses `modalService` for state management **Usage Example:** ```typescript import Modal from 'Components/modal/Modal'; import { ModalNameE } from 'Components/modal/ModalNameE'; // Modal component (rendered in app root) // Open modal from anywhere import { modalService } from 'Services/modalService'; modalService.open(ModalNameE.TRANSACTION); ``` **Dependencies:** `modalService`, `useGlobalValue`, `useOutsideClickListener` **Related Files:** - `ModalNameE.ts` - Enum of modal names - `ModalPropsT.ts` - Type-safe props for each modal - `ModalTypeE.ts` - Modal variants --- ### ErrorModal **Location:** `src/components/modal/errorModal/ErrorModal.tsx` **Purpose:** Displays API error messages in a modal dialog with user-friendly messaging. **Props:** None (uses `useModalData` hook to get message from modal state) **Key Features:** - Uses `useModalData(ModalNameE.ERROR)` to get error message - Displays message in a paragraph with `role="alert"` and `aria-live="assertive"` - Close button dismisses modal via `modalService.close(ModalNameE.ERROR)` - Uses `ModalTypeE.ERROR` for error-specific styling **Usage Example:** ```typescript // ErrorModal is rendered globally in App.tsx // To display an error modal, use modalService: import { modalService } from 'Services/modalService'; import { ModalNameE } from 'Components/modal/ModalNameE'; modalService.open(ModalNameE.ERROR, { message: 'Something went wrong.' }); // To close: modalService.close(ModalNameE.ERROR); ``` **Related Files:** - `errorModal.css` - Error modal styles - `src/shared/requestClient/errorInterceptor.ts` - Triggers modal on API errors **Tests:** - Unit: `ErrorModal.test.tsx` --- ## Sidebar System (`src/components/sidebar/`) ### Sidebar **Location:** `src/components/sidebar/Sidebar.tsx` **Purpose:** Sliding sidebar panel with animations, stacking support, scroll lock management, and mobile swipe-to-close gestures. **Props:** ```typescript type PropsT = { children: ReactNode; // Sidebar content className?: string; headerActions?: ReactNode; // Additional header buttons name: SidebarNameE; // Sidebar identifier onBack?: () => void; // Back button handler onClose?: () => void; // Close callback title?: string; // Sidebar title size?: SidebarSizeE; // REGULAR or other sizes }; ``` **Key Features:** - Renders via React Portal to `#sidebar_portal` - Subscribes to global sidebar state - Supports stacking (multiple sidebars open) - Graceful closing animations via `deleted` flag - Automatically closes on unmount - Closes on backdrop click - Scroll lock for REGULAR size (prevents body scroll) - Back button support - Header actions area - Calls `sidebarService.finalizeClose()` after animation ends - **Mobile swipe-to-close gesture** - Users can swipe down to close sidebar on mobile - **Mobile backdrop overlay** - Semi-transparent backdrop on mobile for better UX - **Dynamic content height** - Mobile sidebar uses `max-height` to adapt to content - **Proper content scrolling** - Sidebar content scrolls smoothly when it exceeds available height **Usage Example:** ```typescript import Sidebar from 'Components/sidebar/Sidebar'; import { SidebarNameE } from 'Components/sidebar/types/SidebarNameE'; import { SidebarSizeE } from 'Components/sidebar/types/SidebarSizeE'; // Sidebar component const EditBudgetSidebar = () => { const sidebars = useGlobalValue(GlobalStoreFieldE.SIDEBAR); const props = sidebars.find(s => s.name === SidebarNameE.EDIT_BUDGET)?.props; if (!props) return null; return ( console.log('Closed')} > ); }; // Open from anywhere import { sidebarService } from 'Services/sidebarService'; sidebarService.openFirst(SidebarNameE.EDIT_BUDGET, { budgetId: 'uuid' }); ``` **Dependencies:** `sidebarService`, `scrollLockService`, `useGlobalValue`, `createPortal` **Related Files:** - `SidebarNameE.ts` - Enum of sidebar names - `SidebarPropsT.ts` - Type-safe props for each sidebar - `SidebarSizeE.ts` - Sidebar size variants - `SidebarLayout.tsx` - Layout wrapper for sidebar content --- ### SidebarLayout **Location:** `src/components/sidebar/SidebarLayout.tsx` **Purpose:** Layout wrapper for sidebar content with consistent structure. **Usage:** Wraps sidebar forms and content for consistent padding and structure. --- ## UI Components ### PageHeader **Location:** `src/components/pageHeader/PageHeader.tsx` **Purpose:** Page header component with title and optional actions. **Props:** ```typescript type PropsT = { children?: React.ReactNode; // Action buttons title: string; // Page title }; ``` **Key Features:** - Displays page title as `

` - Optional actions area on the right - Consistent styling across pages **Usage Example:** ```typescript import PageHeader from 'Components/pageHeader/PageHeader'; import AuButton from 'Components/form/button/AuButton'; New Transaction ``` **Dependencies:** None (pure presentation component) --- ### Spinner **Location:** `src/components/spinner/Spinner.tsx` **Purpose:** Loading spinner component. **Usage:** Display loading state during async operations. --- ### EmptyState **Location:** `src/components/emptyState/EmptyState.tsx` **Purpose:** Empty state placeholder component. **Usage:** Display when lists or data are empty. --- ### CategoryBadge **Location:** `src/components/categoryBage/CategoryBadge.tsx` **Purpose:** Badge component for displaying category with color. **Usage:** Show category in transaction lists and details. --- ### EditableLabel **Location:** `src/components/editableLabel/EditableLabel.tsx` **Purpose:** Inline editable label component. **Usage:** Edit text in place (e.g., budget group names). --- ### ActionDropdown **Location:** `src/components/actionDropdown/ActionDropdown.tsx` **Purpose:** Dropdown menu for row actions. **Usage:** Edit/Delete actions in table rows. --- ## AI Assistant Components (`src/components/aiAssistant/`) ### AIAssistant **Location:** `src/components/aiAssistant/AIAssistant.tsx` **Purpose:** Main AI assistant container with chat bubble and window. **Key Features:** - Toggles between bubble and chat window - Uses `useToggle` hook for state - Positioned fixed on page (via CSS) **Structure:** ```typescript
{isOpen && }
``` **Usage:** Automatically included in `MainLayout`. **Dependencies:** `ChatBubble`, `ChatWindow`, `useToggle` --- ### ChatBubble **Location:** `src/components/aiAssistant/chatBubble/ChatBubble.tsx` **Purpose:** Floating chat bubble button to open AI assistant. **Props:** - `onClick`: Opens chat window - `isOpen`: Current state **Usage:** Rendered in `AIAssistant` component. --- ### ChatWindow **Location:** `src/components/aiAssistant/chatWindow/ChatWindow.tsx` **Purpose:** Chat window for AI assistant conversations. **Props:** - `onClose`: Closes chat window **Key Features:** - Displays conversation history - Input for new messages - Integrates with `aiAssistantService` --- ### MessageContent **Location:** `src/components/aiAssistant/chatWindow/MessageContent.tsx` **Purpose:** Renders individual chat messages. **Usage:** Renders user and assistant messages with formatting. --- ## SVG Icon Components (`src/components/svg/`) **Location:** `src/components/svg/` **Purpose:** icon - `PencilSvg` - Pencil edit icon - `PlusSvg` - Plus icon - `ReloadSVG` - Reload icon - `TrashSvg` - Delete/Trash icon - `TriangleSVG` - Triangle icon - `Svg` - Base SVG wrapper component **Usage Example:** ```typescript import PencilSvg from 'Components/svg/PencilSvg'; import AuButton from 'Components/form/button/AuButton'; import { AuButtonKindE } from 'Components/form/button/AuButtonKindE'; ``` **Key Features:** - Consistent sizing - Themeable via CSS - Accessible with proper aria labels - Can be used in buttons and inline --- ## Component Naming Conventions ### Form Components - **Always** prefix with `Au` - PascalCase: `AuButton`, `AuField`, `AuSelect` - Located in `src/components/form/` ### Page Components - Suffix with `Page` - Example: `TransactionsPage`, `BudgetPage` - Located in `src/pages/` ### Sidebar Components - Suffix with `Sidebar` - Example: `EditBudgetSidebar`, `TransactionFormSidebar` - Located in page directories: `src/pages/budget/sidebars/` ### Layout Components - Suffix with `Layout` - Example: `MainLayout`, `BaseLayout` - Located in `src/components/layouts/` ### SVG Components - Suffix with `Svg` or `SVG` - Example: `PencilSvg`, `CloseSvg` - Located in `src/components/svg/` --- ## Component Styling & Theme Support ### How Component Styling Works All components use SCSS modules with semantic CSS variables for automatic theme support. **Key Principles:** 1. **Semantic Variables Only** - Use `var(--color-text)`, not hardcoded colors 2. **No `!important`** - CSS variables inherit naturally 3. **No Manual Theme Logic** - No `@media (prefers-color-scheme)` in component files 4. **Light Theme First** - Default values at `:root`, overrides in media query ### Semantic Variables Available All components have access to these variables: ```scss // Text colors --color-text // Primary text (black in light, light gray in dark) --color-text-subtle // Secondary text (gray-2 in light, gray-5 in dark) --color-text-muted // Tertiary text (gray-1 in light, gray-6 in dark) // Backgrounds --color-background // Page background (primary-blue-light in light, #0f1419 in dark) --color-foreground // Component background (white in light, #1a1f26 in dark) // Borders --color-border // Primary border (gray-4 in light, gray-7 in dark) --color-border-subtle // Secondary border (gray-3 in light, gray-8 in dark) // Interactive elements --color-button-primary // Button background --color-button-primary-hover // Button hover --color-button-primary-active // Button active/pressed --color-button-text-on-primary // Text on dark button background --color-input-background // Input field background --color-input-border // Input field border --color-input-border-hover // Input on hover --color-input-border-active // Input on focus --color-placeholder // Placeholder text // States --color-disabled-bg // Disabled element background --color-disabled-text // Disabled element text --color-checkbox-unchecked // Checkbox unchecked state --color-avatar-placeholder // Avatar placeholder color // Links --color-link // Link text --color-link-hover // Link on hover ``` See [PATTERNS.md#9-theme-system-pattern](./PATTERNS.md#9-theme-system-pattern) for complete list. ### Example: Styling a Component ```scss // src/components/form/button/auButton.scss .button { // Always use semantic variables background-color: var(--color-button-primary); color: var(--color-button-text-on-primary); border: none; &:hover { background-color: var(--color-button-primary-hover); } &:active { background-color: var(--color-button-primary-active); } &:disabled { background-color: var(--color-disabled-bg); color: var(--color-disabled-text); cursor: not-allowed; } } ``` ### Theme Behavior **Automatic theme switching:** 1. Component uses semantic variables: `background-color: var(--color-button-primary)` 2. Light mode (default): `--color-button-primary: #075eff` (bright blue) 3. Dark mode (OS preference): `--color-button-primary: #5a95ff` (softer blue) 4. No component code changes needed - CSS variables handle it! **Browser updates colors automatically when:** - User changes OS theme preference - User changes browser theme setting - No page refresh required - instant update ### What NOT to Do ```scss // ❌ NEVER use hardcoded colors .button { background-color: #075eff; // WRONG - won't adapt to dark theme } // ❌ NEVER use specific color variables .button { background-color: var(--color-primary-blue); // WRONG - use semantic instead } // ❌ NEVER add @media (prefers-color-scheme) in components // This is handled globally in src/app/styles/variables.scss .button { @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) { background-color: #5a95ff; // WRONG - don't do this } } // ❌ NEVER mix hardcoded colors with variables .button { background-color: var(--color-button-primary); border: 1px solid #cccccc; // WRONG - should use var(--color-border) } ``` --- ## Component Patterns ### 1. Form Integration Pattern Components that integrate with react-hook-form: ```typescript import { useFormContext, useController } from 'react-hook-form'; const MyFormComponent = ({ name }: { name: string }) => { const { control } = useFormContext(); const { field, fieldState: { error } } = useController({ name, control, rules: { required: 'Required' } }); return (
{error && {error.message}}
); }; ``` ### 2. Global State Subscription Pattern Components that read from global state: ```typescript import { useGlobalValue } from 'Shared/store/useGlobalValue'; import { GlobalStoreFieldE } from 'Shared/types/GlobalStoreFieldE'; const MyComponent = () => { const sidebars = useGlobalValue(GlobalStoreFieldE.SIDEBAR); return
{sidebars.length} open
; }; ``` ### 3. Service-Managed UI Pattern Components managed by services (modals, sidebars): ```typescript // Component definition const MySidebar = () => { const sidebars = useGlobalValue(GlobalStoreFieldE.SIDEBAR); const props = sidebars.find(s => s.name === SidebarNameE.MY_SIDEBAR)?.props; if (!props) return null; return ( Content ); }; // Usage - open from anywhere sidebarService.openFirst(SidebarNameE.MY_SIDEBAR, { id: 'uuid' }); ``` ### 4. Portal Pattern Components that render outside their parent DOM hierarchy: ```typescript import { createPortal } from 'react-dom'; const container = document.getElementById('portal_root'); const MyPortalComponent = () => { return createPortal(
Portal Content
, container!, 'unique-key' ); }; ``` --- ## Testing Components Components should have tests in `__tests__` directories adjacent to the component: ``` src/components/form/button/ ├── AuButton.tsx ├── AuButtonKindE.ts ├── AuButtonSizeE.ts ├── auButton.scss └── __tests__/ └── AuButton.test.tsx ``` **Test Guidelines:** - Test user interactions - Test conditional rendering - Test accessibility - Don't test simple prop passing - Use `@testing-library/react` for component tests --- ## Accessibility Guidelines All components should follow these accessibility practices: 1. **Semantic HTML**: Use appropriate HTML elements (` ```

1→# Code Patterns 2→ 3→**Purpose:** Practical guide on how to implement features using established patterns. For understanding why these patterns exist, see [ARCHITECTURE.md](./ARCHITECTURE.md). 4→ 5→--- 6→ 7→## 1. Modal Pattern 8→ 9→### How It Works 10→ 11→Modals are managed through `modalService` which uses global state to control modal lifecycle. Each modal has a name (enum) and optional typed props. 12→ 13→**Service Methods:** 14→ 15→- `open(modalName, props?)` - Opens a modal with optional props 16→- `close(modalName)` - Closes a specific modal 17→ 18→**Implementation:** 19→ 20→- Modals stack on top of each other 21→- Props are type-safe based on modal name 22→- State stored in `GlobalStoreFieldE.MODALS` 23→ 24→### When to Use 25→ 26→- Confirmation dialogs 27→- Small forms or quick actions 28→- Alerts and notifications requiring user acknowledgment 29→ 30→### Usage Example 31→ 32→```typescript 33→import { modalService } from 'Services/modalService'; 34→import { ModalNameE } from 'Components/modal/ModalNameE'; 35→ 36→// Open modal with props 37→modalService.open(ModalNameE.TRANSACTION, { transactionId: 'uuid' }); 38→ 39→// Close modal 40→modalService.close(ModalNameE.TRANSACTION); 41→``` 42→ 43→### Example Files 44→ 45→- Service: `src/services/modalService.ts` 46→- Modal names: `src/components/modal/ModalNameE.ts` 47→- Modal props: `src/components/modal/ModalPropsT.ts` 48→- Modal component: `src/components/modal/Modal.tsx` 49→ 50→--- 51→ 52→## 2. Sidebar Pattern 53→ 54→### How It Works 55→ 56→Sidebars are managed through `sidebarService` with support for stacking and graceful animations. Each sidebar has a name (enum) and typed props. 57→ 58→**Service Methods:** 59→ 60→- `open(sidebarName, props?)` - Opens sidebar, adds to stack (removes duplicate if exists) 61→- `openFirst(sidebarName, props?)` - Opens sidebar, clears entire stack first 62→- `close(sidebarName)` - Marks sidebar as deleted (triggers close animation) 63→- `closeAll()` - Marks all sidebars as deleted 64→- `finalizeClose(sidebarName)` - Removes sidebar from state after animation completes 65→ 66→**Implementation:** 67→ 68→- Sidebars support stacking (multiple open at once) 69→- Graceful closing with animations via `deleted` flag 70→- Props are type-safe based on sidebar name 71→- State stored in `GlobalStoreFieldE.SIDEBAR` 72→ 73→### When to Use 74→ 75→- Forms (create, edit) 76→- Detail views 77→- Multi-step workflows 78→- Delete confirmations 79→- Any panel that slides in from the side 80→ 81→### Usage Example 82→ 83→```typescript 84→import { sidebarService } from 'Services/sidebarService'; 85→import { SidebarNameE } from 'Components/sidebar/types/SidebarNameE'; 86→ 87→// Open sidebar and clear stack 88→sidebarService.openFirst(SidebarNameE.EDIT_BUDGET_GROUP, { 89→ budgetGroup: budgetGroup, 90→}); 91→ 92→// Open sidebar and add to stack 93→sidebarService.open(SidebarNameE.CREATE_BUDGET_ITEM); 94→ 95→// Close specific sidebar (triggers animation) 96→sidebarService.close(SidebarNameE.EDIT_BUDGET_GROUP); 97→ 98→// Close all sidebars 99→sidebarService.closeAll(); 100→ 101→// Finalize close after animation (called by Sidebar component) 102→sidebarService.finalizeClose(SidebarNameE.EDIT_BUDGET_GROUP); 103→``` 104→ 105→### Example Files 106→ 107→- Service: `src/services/sidebarService.ts` 108→- Sidebar names: `src/components/sidebar/types/SidebarNameE.ts` 109→- Sidebar props: `src/components/sidebar/types/SidebarPropsT.ts` 110→- Sidebar component: `src/components/sidebar/Sidebar.tsx` 111→- Usage example: `src/pages/budget/group/BudgetGroup.tsx:72` 112→ 113→--- 114→ 115→## 3. State Management Pattern 116→ 117→### How It Works 118→ 119→Global state uses `@preact/signals-react` for reactive state management. State is defined in `globalStore` and accessed/updated through utilities. 120→ 121→**Core Components:** 122→ 123→- `globalStore` - Central store with signals for each field 124→- `useGlobalValue(field)` - Hook to read state in components 125→- `createGlobalValueSetter(field)` - Creates updater function for services 126→ 127→**Implementation:** 128→ 129→- Each field is a `signal` that can be subscribed to 130→- React components subscribe using `useGlobalValue` 131→- Services update state using setter functions 132→- Changes automatically trigger re-renders in subscribed components 133→ 134→### When to Use 135→ 136→- Auth token 137→- Notifications 138→- Modals and sidebars state 139→- IDs lists (e.g., deleted items for optimistic updates) 140→- Any state shared across multiple components 141→ 142→### Usage Example 143→ 144→```typescript 145→// In a service 146→import { createGlobalValueSetter } from 'Shared/store/createGlobalValueSetter'; 147→import { GlobalStoreFieldE } from 'Shared/types/GlobalStoreFieldE'; 148→ 149→const updateNotifications = createGlobalValueSetter( 150→ GlobalStoreFieldE.NOTIFICATIONS, 151→); 152→ 153→export const notificationService = { 154→ add: notification => { 155→ updateNotifications(current => [...current, notification]); 156→ }, 157→}; 158→ 159→// In a component 160→import { useGlobalValue } from 'Shared/store/useGlobalValue'; 161→import { GlobalStoreFieldE } from 'Shared/types/GlobalStoreFieldE'; 162→ 163→const MyComponent = () => { 164→ const notifications = useGlobalValue(GlobalStoreFieldE.NOTIFICATIONS); 165→ 166→ return
{notifications.length} notifications
; 167→}; 168→``` 169→ 170→### Example Files 171→ 172→- Store: `src/shared/store/globalStore.ts` 173→- Hook: `src/shared/store/useGlobalValue.ts` 174→- Setter: `src/shared/store/createGlobalValueSetter.ts` 175→- Field names: `src/shared/types/GlobalStoreFieldE.ts` 176→- Usage in service: `src/services/sidebarService.ts:6` 177→ 178→--- 179→ 180→## 4. Data Fetching Pattern 181→ 182→### How It Works 183→ 184→Data fetching uses `@tanstack/react-query` with repository hooks that wrap API calls. Each repository hook handles a specific operation. 185→ 186→**Naming Convention:** 187→ 188→- `useLoadX` - Fetch single item or list 189→- `useCreateX` - Create new item 190→- `useEditX` - Update existing item 191→- `useDeleteX` - Delete item 192→ 193→**Implementation:** 194→ 195→- All API calls go through `requestClient` (Axios wrapper) 196→- Hooks use `useQuery` (read) or `useMutation` (write) 197→- Query keys based on `ApiGatewayE` enum 198→- Automatic cache invalidation after mutations 199→- Infinite queries for paginated lists 200→ 201→### When to Use 202→ 203→- All API interactions 204→- Server state management 205→- Caching and background updates 206→- Optimistic updates 207→ 208→### Query Hook Example (Read) 209→ 210→```typescript 211→import { useInfiniteQuery } from '@tanstack/react-query'; 212→import { requestClient } from 'Shared/requestClient/requestClient'; 213→import { ApiGatewayE } from 'Shared/types/api/ApiGatewayE'; 214→ 215→export const useTransactionList = filters => { 216→ return useInfiniteQuery({ 217→ queryKey: [ApiGatewayE.BANK_TRANSACTIONS, filters], 218→ queryFn: async ({ pageParam = 1 }) => { 219→ return await requestClient.get(ApiGatewayE.BANK_TRANSACTIONS, undefined, { 220→ page: pageParam, 221→ ...filters, 222→ }); 223→ }, 224→ getNextPageParam: lastPage => { 225→ return lastPage.page < lastPage.totalPages ? lastPage.page + 1 : undefined; 226→ }, 227→ }); 228→}; 229→ 230→// In component - destructure what you need 231→const MyComponent = () => { 232→ const { data, isLoading, fetchNextPage, hasNextPage } = 233→ useTransactionList(filters); 234→ 235→ const transactions = data?.pages.flatMap(p => p.results) ?? []; 236→ 237→ return ; 238→}; 239→``` 240→ 241→### Mutation Hook Example (Write) 242→ 243→```typescript 244→import { useMutation } from '@tanstack/react-query'; 245→import { queryClient } from 'Shared/requestClient/queryClient'; 246→import { requestClient } from 'Shared/requestClient/requestClient'; 247→import { ApiGatewayE } from 'Shared/types/api/ApiGatewayE'; 248→ 249→export const useCreateTransaction = () => { 250→ const mutation = useMutation({ 251→ mutationFn: async data => { 252→ return await requestClient.post( 253→ ApiGatewayE.BANK_TRANSACTIONS, 254→ undefined, 255→ data, 256→ ); 257→ }, 258→ }); 259→ 260→ return async transactionBody => { 261→ await mutation.mutateAsync(transactionBody); 262→ 263→ // Invalidate cache to refetch 264→ await queryClient.invalidateQueries({ 265→ queryKey: [ApiGatewayE.BANK_TRANSACTIONS], 266→ }); 267→ }; 268→}; 269→ 270→// In component 271→const MyComponent = () => { 272→ const createTransaction = useCreateTransaction(); 273→ 274→ const handleCreate = async data => { 275→ try { 276→ await createTransaction(data); 277→ notificationService.addAlert('Transaction created!'); 278→ } catch (error) { 279→ notificationService.addErrorAlert(error.message); 280→ } 281→ }; 282→ 283→ return ; 284→}; 285→``` 286→ 287→### Example Files 288→ 289→- Query hook: `src/repositories/transactions/useTransactionList.ts` 290→- Mutation hook: `src/repositories/transactions/useCreateTransaction.ts` 291→- Request client: `src/shared/requestClient/requestClient.ts` 292→- Query client: `src/shared/requestClient/queryClient.ts` 293→- API endpoints: `src/shared/types/api/ApiGatewayE.ts` 294→ 295→--- 296→ 297→## 5. Form Components Pattern 298→ 299→### How It Works 300→ 301→Form components follow the `Au*` naming convention (Atomic UI). All form-related components are prefixed with `Au`. 302→ 303→**Available Components:** 304→ 305→- `AuButton` - Button component 306→- `AuField` - Form field wrapper (label + input + error) 307→- `AuDropdown` - Dropdown menu 308→- `AuSelect` - Select dropdown 309→- `AuCheckbox` - Checkbox input 310→- `AuRadioGroup` - Radio button group 311→- `AuDatePicker` - Date picker 312→- `AuForm` - Form container 313→- `AuFormBlock` - Form section wrapper 314→ 315→**Implementation:** 316→ 317→- Consistent API across all form components 318→- Built-in error handling and validation 319→- Accessible by default 320→- Styled with SCSS modules 321→ 322→### When to Use 323→ 324→- All form inputs and controls 325→- Interactive UI elements (buttons, toggles) 326→- Form layouts and sections 327→ 328→### Naming Rules 329→ 330→- **Always** prefix form components with `Au` 331→- Use PascalCase after prefix: `AuButton`, not `Aubutton` 332→- Place in `src/components/form/` directory 333→- Create subdirectory for each component: `form/button/AuButton.tsx` 334→ 335→### Usage Example 336→ 337→```typescript 338→import AuButton from 'Components/form/button/AuButton'; 339→import AuField from 'Components/form/field/AuField'; 340→import { AuButtonKindE } from 'Components/form/button/AuButtonKindE'; 341→import { validationPatterns } from 'Shared/constants/validationPatterns'; 342→import { FormProvider, useForm } from 'react-hook-form'; 343→ 344→const MyForm = () => { 345→ const formMethods = useForm({ 346→ defaultValues: { email: '', amount: '' }, 347→ }); 348→ 349→ const handleSubmit = async data => { 350→ // Handle form submission 351→ }; 352→ 353→ return ( 354→ 355→
356→ 357→ 358→ 359→ 360→ 366→ 367→ 368→ 369→ 370→ Save 371→ 372→
373→ 374→ ); 375→}; 376→``` 377→ 378→### Example Files 379→ 380→- Components: `src/components/form/*/Au*.tsx` 381→- Button: `src/components/form/button/AuButton.tsx` 382→- Field: `src/components/form/field/AuField.tsx` 383→- Dropdown: `src/components/form/dropdown/AuDropdown.tsx` 384→ 385→--- 386→ 387→## 6. Routing Pattern 388→ 389→### How It Works 390→ 391→Routing uses React Router v7 with Sentry integration. Routes are organized with route guards for authentication. 392→ 393→**Route Guards:** 394→ 395→- `OnlyPrivate` - Wraps authenticated routes, redirects to login if not authenticated 396→- `OnlyPublic` - Wraps public routes (login, registration), redirects to app if authenticated 397→ 398→**Implementation:** 399→ 400→- Routes defined in `AppRoutes.tsx` using `createRoutesFromElements` 401→- Sentry wraps router for error tracking 402→- Path constants defined in `appPaths` using `AppPath` class 403→- Nested route structure with guards as parent routes 404→ 405→### When to Use 406→ 407→- Adding new pages 408→- Protecting authenticated routes 409→- Defining navigation paths 410→ 411→### Usage Example 412→ 413→```typescript 414→// In appPaths.ts 415→export const appPaths = { 416→ TRANSACTIONS: new AppPath('/transactions'), 417→ BUDGET: new AppPath('/budgets/:budgetId'), 418→}; 419→ 420→// In AppRoutes.tsx 421→}> 422→ } /> 423→ } /> 424→ 425→ 426→}> 427→ } /> 428→ 429→ 430→// In component - navigate programmatically 431→import { useNavigate } from 'react-router-dom'; 432→import { appPaths } from 'Shared/constants/appPaths'; 433→ 434→const MyComponent = () => { 435→ const navigate = useNavigate(); 436→ 437→ const goToBudget = budgetId => { 438→ navigate(appPaths.BUDGET.getPath({ budgetId })); 439→ }; 440→ 441→ return ; 442→}; 443→``` 444→ 445→### Example Files 446→ 447→- Routes: `src/app/routes/AppRoutes.tsx` 448→- Route guards: `src/app/routes/OnlyPrivate.tsx`, `src/app/routes/OnlyPublic.tsx` 449→- Path constants: `src/shared/constants/appPaths.ts` 450→- Path helper: `src/shared/utils/url/AppPath.ts` 451→ 452→--- 453→ 454→## 7. Path Aliases 455→ 456→### How It Works 457→ 458→TypeScript path aliases provide absolute imports instead of relative paths. Configured in `tsconfig.json` and `vite.config.ts`. 459→ 460→**Available Aliases:** 461→ 462→- `Assets/*` → `src/assets/*` 463→- `Repositories/*` → `src/repositories/*` 464→- `Services/*` → `src/services/*` 465→- `Components/*` → `src/components/*` 466→- `Shared/*` → `src/shared/*` 467→ 468→**Important:** Both TypeScript and Vite configs must be kept in sync. 469→ 470→### When to Use 471→ 472→- **Always** use path aliases for imports 473→- **Never** use relative paths like `../../../` 474→- Makes refactoring easier 475→- Improves code readability 476→ 477→### Usage Example 478→ 479→```typescript 480→// ✅ GOOD - Use path aliases 481→import { sidebarService } from 'Services/sidebarService'; 482→import { useTransactionList } from 'Repositories/transactions/useTransactionList'; 483→import AuButton from 'Components/form/button/AuButton'; 484→import { TransactionDTO } from 'Shared/types/TransactionDTO'; 485→import logo from 'Assets/logo.svg'; 486→ 487→// ❌ BAD - Avoid relative paths 488→import { sidebarService } from '../../../services/sidebarService'; 489→import { useTransactionList } from '../../repositories/transactions/useTransactionList'; 490→import AuButton from '../../../components/form/button/AuButton'; 491→``` 492→ 493→### Configuration Files 494→ 495→- TypeScript config: `tsconfig.json` 496→- Vite config: `vite.config.ts` 497→ 498→--- 499→ 500→## 8. API Error Handling Pattern 501→ 502→### How It Works 503→ 504→API errors are handled globally through axios response interceptors. Different error types are handled differently: 505→ 506→1. **ValidationError (400)** - Form field validation errors shown inline 507→2. **AuthorizationError (401)** - Triggers logout, redirects to login 508→3. **Other API Errors** - Display in error modal with user-friendly message 509→ 510→**Implementation:** 511→ 512→- Global interceptor in `axiosClient.ts` catches all API errors 513→- `errorInterceptor.ts` handles displaying error modal 514→- ErrorModal component renders globally in App.tsx 515→- modalService manages error modal state 516→ 517→### When to Use 518→ 519→- All API errors that should notify the user 520→- Server errors (500) 521→- Network errors 522→- Any error not handled by ValidationError or AuthorizationError 523→ 524→### Usage Example 525→ 526→```typescript 527→// Automatic - errors are caught globally by axios interceptor 528→// No manual error handling needed for API errors 529→ 530→const handleSave = async () => { 531→ // If this fails with 500, error modal appears automatically 532→ await requestClient.post(ApiGatewayE.BUDGETS, undefined, budgetData); 533→ notificationService.addAlert('Budget saved!'); 534→}; 535→ 536→// Manual - if you need to show a specific error message 537→import { modalService } from 'Services/modalService'; 538→import { ModalNameE } from 'Components/modal/ModalNameE'; 539→ 540→modalService.open(ModalNameE.ERROR, { message: 'Custom error message' }); 541→``` 542→ 543→### Error Message Extraction 544→ 545→The interceptor extracts user-friendly messages in this order: 546→ 547→1. `response.data.message` (string) 548→2. `response.data.errors[0].msg` (validation format) 549→3. Fallback: `'Something went wrong. Please try again.'` 550→ 551→### Example Files 552→ 553→- Error interceptor: `src/shared/requestClient/errorInterceptor.ts` 554→- Axios client: `src/shared/requestClient/axiosClient.ts` 555→- Error modal component: `src/components/modal/errorModal/ErrorModal.tsx` 556→- Error modal styles: `src/components/modal/errorModal/errorModal.css` 557→ 558→--- 559→ 560→## 9. Theme System Pattern 561→ 562→### How It Works 563→ 564→The application automatically detects and applies theme colors based on the user's browser or operating system preference via the CSS media query `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)`. 565→ 566→**Implementation:** 567→ 568→- Theme variables defined in `src/app/styles/variables.scss` 569→- Light theme: Default CSS variables at `:root` 570→- Dark theme: Overridden variables in `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) :root` 571→- Semantic color variables used throughout all components 572→- No manual theme toggle - theme switches automatically with OS/browser settings 573→ 574→### Semantic Color Variables 575→ 576→All components use semantic variables instead of specific colors. This ensures theme consistency. 577→ 578→**Light Theme Semantic Variables:** 579→ 580→```scss 581→--color-background: var(--color-primary-blue-light); 582→--color-foreground: #fff; 583→--color-text: #000; 584→--color-text-subtle: var(--color-gray-2); 585→--color-text-muted: var(--color-gray-1); 586→--color-border: var(--color-gray-4); 587→--color-border-subtle: var(--color-gray-3); 588→--color-placeholder: var(--color-gray-3); 589→--color-disabled-bg: var(--color-gray-4); 590→--color-disabled-text: var(--color-gray-3); 591→``` 592→ 593→**Dark Theme Semantic Variables:** 594→ 595→```scss 596→@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) { 597→ :root { 598→ --color-background: #0f1419; 599→ --color-foreground: #1a1f26; 600→ --color-text: #e0e0e0; 601→ --color-text-subtle: var(--color-gray-5); 602→ --color-text-muted: var(--color-gray-6); 603→ --color-border: var(--color-gray-7); 604→ --color-border-subtle: var(--color-gray-8); 605→ --color-placeholder: var(--color-gray-8); 606→ --color-disabled-bg: var(--color-gray-7); 607→ --color-disabled-text: var(--color-gray-6); 608→ } 609→} 610→``` 611→ 612→### Brand Color Adjustments 613→ 614→Brand blue colors are softened in dark mode for better contrast and readability: 615→ 616→**Light Mode:** 617→ 618→- `--color-primary-blue: #075eff` (bright, saturated blue) 619→- `--color-primary-blue-darker: #0640ac` 620→- `--color-primary-blue-light: #f3f7ff` 621→ 622→**Dark Mode:** 623→ 624→- `--color-primary-blue: #5a95ff` (softer, less saturated) 625→- `--color-primary-blue-darker: #7eabff` (lighter for contrast) 626→- `--color-primary-blue-light: #1a2a3d` (dark background instead of light) 627→ 628→### When to Use 629→ 630→- Use semantic variables for all color styling (never hardcoded colors) 631→- Use specific colors only for brand colors in variables.scss 632→- All SCSS files should reference semantic variables 633→ 634→### Usage Example 635→ 636→```scss 637→// SCSS file - use semantic variables only 638→.button { 639→ background-color: var(--color-button-primary); 640→ color: var(--color-button-text-on-primary); 641→ border: 1px solid var(--color-border); 642→ 643→ &:hover { 644→ background-color: var(--color-button-primary-hover); 645→ } 646→ 647→ &:disabled { 648→ background-color: var(--color-disabled-bg); 649→ color: var(--color-disabled-text); 650→ } 651→} 652→ 653→.text-subtle { 654→ color: var(--color-text-subtle); 655→} 656→ 657→.input { 658→ background-color: var(--color-foreground); 659→ color: var(--color-text); 660→ border: 1px solid var(--color-input-border); 661→ 662→ &::placeholder { 663→ color: var(--color-placeholder); 664→ } 665→} 666→``` 667→ 668→### Theme Switching 669→ 670→Theme switching happens automatically - no code needed: 671→ 672→1. User changes OS/browser theme preference 673→2. Browser applies matching `prefers-color-scheme` media query 674→3. CSS variables automatically update 675→4. All components re-render with new colors 676→ 677→**No manual theme toggle or localStorage required.** 678→ 679→### Example Files 680→ 681→- Variables: `src/app/styles/variables.scss` 682→- Helpers: `src/app/styles/helpers.scss` 683→- Component example: `src/components/form/button/auButton.scss` 684→ 685→--- 686→ 687→## Summary 688→ 689→### Key Principles 690→ 691→1. **Services manage UI state** - Use modalService, sidebarService for modals/sidebars 692→2. **Type-safe props** - Enums + mapped types ensure correct props at compile time 693→3. **Repository pattern** - All API calls go through repository hooks 694→4. **Absolute imports** - Always use path aliases 695→5. **Naming conventions** - Au* for forms, *Page for pages, \*Sidebar for sidebars 696→6. **Route guards** - Protect routes with OnlyPrivate/OnlyPublic 697→ 698→--- 699→ 700→## Common Workflows 701→ 702→### Opening a sidebar with form 703→ 704→```typescript 705→import { sidebarService } from 'Services/sidebarService'; 830→ 831→ ); 832→}; 833→``` 834→ 835→--- 836→ 837→## 10. Custom Hooks Pattern 838→ 839→### How It Works 840→ 841→Custom hooks encapsulate reusable logic that can be used across components. They follow React hooks conventions and can manage state, effects, and event listeners. 842→ 843→**Naming Convention:** 844→ 845→- Prefix with `use`: `useSwipeGesture`, `useToggle`, `useLocalStorage` 846→- Describe what the hook does clearly 847→ 848→**Implementation:** 849→ 850→- Use `useRef` for mutable values that don't trigger re-renders 851→- Use `useEffect` for side effects with proper dependency arrays 852→- Always clean up event listeners and subscriptions 853→- Return object with ref or functions for component integration 854→ 855→### When to Use 856→ 857→- Logic that needs to be reused across multiple components 858→- Encapsulating event listener setup/cleanup 859→- Managing component state and lifecycle 860→- Wrapping repetitive patterns 861→ 862→### Usage Example 863→ 864→```typescript 865→// Definition 866→const useSwipeGesture = ({ 867→ onSwipeDown, 868→ threshold = 100, 869→ enabled = true, 870→}: SwipeGestureOptionsT): UseSwipeGestureReturnT => { 871→ const ref = useRef(null); 872→ const startY = useRef(0); 873→ const isDragging = useRef(false); 874→ 875→ useEffect(() => { 876→ const element = ref.current; 877→ if (!element || !enabled) return; 878→ 879→ const handleTouchStart = (e: TouchEvent) => { 880→ const touch = e.touches[0]; 881→ if (!touch) return; 882→ startY.current = touch.clientY; 883→ isDragging.current = true; 884→ if (element) element.style.transition = 'none'; 885→ }; 886→ 887→ const handleTouchMove = (e: TouchEvent) => { 888→ if (!isDragging.current) return; 889→ const touch = e.touches[0]; 890→ if (!touch) return; 891→ const deltaY = touch.clientY - startY.current; 892→ if (deltaY > 0) { 893→ if (element) element.style.transform = `translateY(${deltaY}px)`; 894→ e.preventDefault(); 895→ } 896→ }; 897→ 898→ const handleTouchEnd = (e: TouchEvent) => { 899→ if (!isDragging.current) return; 900→ const touch = e.changedTouches[0]; 901→ if (!touch) return; 902→ const deltaY = touch.clientY - startY.current; 903→ if (element) { 904→ element.style.transition = ''; 905→ element.style.transform = ''; 906→ } 907→ if (deltaY > threshold) onSwipeDown(); 908→ isDragging.current = false; 909→ startY.current = 0; 910→ }; 911→ 912→ element.addEventListener('touchstart', handleTouchStart, { passive: false }); 913→ element.addEventListener('touchmove', handleTouchMove, { passive: false }); 914→ element.addEventListener('touchend', handleTouchEnd); 915→ 916→ return () => { 917→ element.removeEventListener('touchstart', handleTouchStart); 918→ element.removeEventListener('touchmove', handleTouchMove); 919→ element.removeEventListener('touchend', handleTouchEnd); 920→ }; 921→ }, [enabled, threshold, onSwipeDown]); 922→ 923→ return { ref }; 924→}; 925→ 926→// Usage 927→const MyComponent = () => { 928→ const { ref } = useSwipeGesture({ 929→ onSwipeDown: () => handleClose(), 930→ threshold: 100, 931→ enabled: isMobile, 932→ }); 933→ 934→ return
Swipeable content
; 935→}; 936→``` 937→ 938→### Key Patterns 939→ 940→**Using refs for mutable state:** 941→ 942→- Use `useRef` when you need to store values that don't affect rendering 943→- Update refs directly without triggering re-renders 944→- Useful for tracking flags (isDragging) and previous values (startY) 945→ 946→**Event listener cleanup:** 947→ 948→- Always remove listeners in cleanup function 949→- Prevents memory leaks and multiple listeners 950→- Dependencies must include all captured values 951→ 952→**Conditional logic:** 953→ 954→- Check `enabled` prop to toggle hook functionality 955→- Guard against null elements before using them 956→- Check for touch events before accessing properties 957→ 958→### Example Files 959→ 960→- Custom hooks: `src/shared/hooks/` 961→- useSwipeGesture: `src/shared/hooks/useSwipeGesture.ts` 962→- useSwipeGesture tests: `src/shared/hooks/__tests__/useSwipeGesture.test.ts` 963→ 964→--- 965→ 966→## For Agents 967→ 968→When implementing features: 969→ 970→1. **Check this doc first** - Find the relevant pattern 971→2. **Copy working examples** - Use code from this guide 972→3. **Follow naming conventions** - Consistency is key 973→4. **Use path aliases** - Never relative imports 974→5. **Test the pattern** - Ensure it works before moving on 975→ 976→If you need to understand _why_ a pattern exists, see [ARCHITECTURE.md](./ARCHITECTURE.md). 977→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

content.thinking:
Теперь у меня есть полная картина:

## Что уже есть:
1. **4 агента**: BA, Developer, Tester, Documenter
2. **Команды**: /task:create, /task:quickfix, /pr:create, /pr:review
3. **Документация**: PATTERNS, COMPONENTS, SERVICES, ARCHITECTURE, BUSINESS_RULES, PROJECT_STRUCTURE
4. **Hooks**: Auto-format (prettier), auto-stage (git add)

## Что нужно для надежной работы с фронтендом:

### Проблемы текущего workflow:
1. **Нет генератора компонентов** - каждый раз нужно создавать структуру вручную
2. **Нет проверки стилей** - stylelint не настроен
3. **Нет визуальной проверки** - нет инструментов для проверки UI
4. **Нет автотестов для компонентов** - только ручное написание
5. **Нет проверки accessibility** - нет a11y аудита
6. **Нет проверки bundle size** - нет отслеживания размера

### Идеи для инструментов:

1. **Component Scaffold** (`/component:create`)
   - Создает структуру: Component.tsx, component.scss, __tests__/Component.test.tsx
   - Следует паттернам из COMPONENTS.md
   - Добавляет в index.ts

2. **Style Audit** (`/style:audit`)
   - Проверяет использование semantic variables
   - Находит hardcoded colors
   - Проверяет mobile-first подход

3. **A11y Audit** (`/a11y:audit`)
   Инструмент для комплексной проверки доступности интерфейса. Он анализирует корректность использования ARIA-меток, оценивает возможность навигации с клавиатуры и проверяет семантическую структуру HTML. Цель - обеспечить максимальную доступность для пользователей с особенностями здоровья.

4. **Test Generator** (`/test:generate`)
   Автоматизированный генератор тестов, который создает базовый набор тестовых сценариев для компонентов. Использует подходы testing-library для написания понятных и эффективных тестов, покрывающих основные варианты использования компонента.

5. **Repository Hook Generator** (`/repo:create`)
   Утилита для быстрого создания React Query хуков с типовыми паттернами загрузки, создания, редактирования и удаления сущностей. Стандартизирует подход к работе с данными на фронтенде.

6. **Visual Review** - агент для проверки визуальных аспектов интерфейса, обеспечивающий целостность дизайна и пользовательского опыта.

Визуальный анализ дизайна с точки зрения соответствия макету. Проверяет адаптивное поведение интерфейса на разных устройствах, гарантируя корректность отображения.

7. **Performance Check** (`/perf:check`)
   Инструмент для оптимизации производительности приложения. Анализирует размер бандла, выявляет избыточные импорты и оценивает эффективность повторных рендеров компонентов.

Подготовка уточняющих вопросов для определения приоритетов проекта.

## Резюме исследования

- **4 агента**: BA → Developer → Tester → Documenter
- **Команды**: `/task:create`, `/task:quickfix`, `/pr:create`, `/pr:review`

### Что я нашёл в лучших практиках:
1. **Multi-Claude Verification** - отдельные инстансы для написания и ревью кода
2. **Модульные инструменты** - 42 инструмента + 15 workflow в [wshobson/commands](https://github.com/wshobson/commands)
3. **Специализированные агенты** - accessibility-audit, security-scan, test-harness

| `/style:audit` | Проверка SCSS (semantic vars, mobile-first) | ⬇️ меньше стилевых багов |
| `/a11y:audit` | Accessibility проверка | ⬇️ меньше a11y проблем |
| `/test:generate` | Генерация тестов для компонента | ⬇️ покрытие тестами |
| `/repo:create` | Scaffold React Query хуков | ⬇️ consistency |
| `/visual:check` | Визуальная проверка компонентов | ⬇️ UI баги |
| `/perf:check` | Bundle size и re-renders | ⬇️ performance issues |

input.prompt:
Design a detailed implementation plan for 3 Claude Code tools for frontend development.

## Context

**Project**: React 19 + TypeScript + Vite app with existing multi-agent system (BA, Developer, Tester, Documenter)

**Existing patterns** (from PATTERNS.md and COMPONENTS.md):
- Components in `src/components/` with structure: Component.tsx, component.scss, types/
- Form components prefixed with `Au` (AuButton, AuField)
- Page components suffixed with `Page`
- Sidebar components suffixed with `Sidebar`
- SVG icons suffixed with `Svg`
- Props type always named `PropsT`
- Path aliases: Components/, Services/, Shared/
- Tests in `__tests__/` directory next to component
- SCSS with semantic CSS variables (no hardcoded colors)
- Mobile-first responsive design

**User requirements**:
- Each tool as separate slash command (not integrated into existing workflow)
- User has Figma designs for visual check
- Tools should reduce review time and increase reliability

## Tools to Design

### 1. `/component:create <name> [--type page|sidebar|form|svg]`
Scaffold a new component following project patterns.

Requirements:
- Create folder structure matching existing patterns
- Generate tsx, scss, types if needed
- Add to appropriate location based on type
- Follow naming conventions (PropsT, Au* prefix for forms)
- Include basic accessibility attributes
- Generate skeleton test file

### 2. `/test:generate <component-path>`
Generate tests for an existing component.

Requirements:
- Analyze component and its props
- Generate test file with testing-library patterns
- Cover: rendering, interactions, conditional rendering
- Follow existing test patterns in codebase
- Place in __tests__/ next to component

### 3. `/visual:check [component-path|--all]`
Visual review agent to check UI implementation.

Requirements:
- User provides Figma link or screenshot
- Agent takes screenshots of component (how? via Playwright?)
- Compares implementation with design
- Reports differences: spacing, colors, responsiveness
- Checks mobile and desktop views

## Deliverable

For each tool, provide:
1. Command signature and options
2. File to create (slash command markdown)
3. Key implementation steps
4. Dependencies or prerequisites
5. Example usage
6. How it reduces review time

Consider:
- How to handle Figma integration (MCP server? WebFetch? Screenshot comparison?)
- Whether to create agents or just slash commands
- Error handling and edge cases

input.content:
# Frontend Development Tools for Claude Code

## Overview

Create 3 standalone slash commands to improve frontend development reliability and reduce review time.

## Tools to Create

### 1. `/component:create <name> [--type page|sidebar|form|svg]`

**Purpose:** Scaffold components following project patterns

**Location:** `client/.claude/commands/component:create.md`

**Features:**
- Type-aware scaffolding (page, sidebar, form, svg, generic)
- Auto-applies naming conventions (Au*, *Page, *Sidebar, *Svg)
- Generates: tsx, scss, types/, __tests__/ skeleton
- Auto-registers sidebars in SidebarNameE + SidebarPropsT
- Includes ARIA attributes by default

**Output locations by type:**
| Type | Path | Naming |
|------|------|--------|
| page | `src/pages/{name}/` | `{Name}Page.tsx` |
| sidebar | `src/pages/{context}/sidebars/` | `{Name}Sidebar.tsx` |
| form | `src/components/form/{name}/` | `Au{Name}.tsx` |
| svg | `src/components/svg/` | `{Name}Svg.tsx` |
| generic | `src/components/{name}/` | `{Name}.tsx` |

---

### 2. `/test:generate <component-path>`

**Purpose:** Generate tests for existing components

**Location:** `client/.claude/commands/test:generate.md`

**Features:**
- Analyzes component: PropsT, hooks, event handlers, conditionals
- Generates Vitest + Testing Library tests
- Follows existing test patterns in codebase
- Auto-mocks common dependencies
- Places in `__tests__/` next to component

**Test categories generated:**
- Rendering tests
- Props tests
- Interaction tests (onClick, onSubmit, etc.)
- Conditional rendering tests
- Hook behavior tests

---

### 3. `/visual:check [component-path] [--figma url] [--screenshot path]`

**Purpose:** Visual review comparing implementation to Figma designs

**Location:** `client/.claude/commands/visual:check.md`

**Features:**
- Captures screenshots via Playwright at mobile (375px) and desktop (1440px)
- User provides Figma link or screenshot as reference
- Claude compares images using vision capabilities
- Reports: color mismatches, spacing issues, responsive problems
- Validates CSS semantic variables usage

**Output:** Structured report with Critical/Minor findings and CSS suggestions

---

## Implementation Order

1. **`/component:create`** - Most straightforward, template-based
2. **`/test:generate`** - Requires component analysis logic
3. **`/visual:check`** - Most complex, needs Playwright + vision integration

## Files to Modify

**New files:**
- `client/.claude/commands/component:create.md`
- `client/.claude/commands/test:generate.md`
- `client/.claude/commands/visual:check.md`

**Reference files (read-only):**
- `client/.claude/commands/task:create.md` - Command pattern
- `client/.claude/docs/PATTERNS.md` - Component patterns
- `client/.claude/docs/COMPONENTS.md` - Naming conventions
- `client/src/components/sidebar/types/SidebarNameE.ts` - Sidebar enum
- `client/src/components/sidebar/types/SidebarPropsT.ts` - Sidebar props
- `client/playwright.config.ts` - Playwright setup

## Dependencies

- Playwright already installed (for visual:check)
- Vitest + Testing Library already configured (for test:generate)
- No new dependencies needed

## Review Time Reduction

| Tool | Reduces |
|------|---------|
| component:create | Boilerplate errors, naming mistakes, missing registrations |
| test:generate | Missing tests, inconsistent test structure |
| visual:check | UI discrepancies, hardcoded colors, responsive bugs |

### 1. `/component:create <name> [--type page|sidebar|form|svg]`

**Purpose:** Scaffold components following project patterns

**Location:** `client/.claude/commands/component:create.md`

**Features:**
- Type-aware scaffolding (page, sidebar, form, svg, generic)
- Auto-applies naming conventions (Au*, *Page, *Sidebar, *Svg)
- Generates: tsx, scss, types/, __tests__/ skeleton
- Auto-registers sidebars in SidebarNameE + SidebarPropsT
- Includes ARIA attributes by default

**Output locations by type:**
| Type | Path | Naming |
|------|------|--------|
| page | `src/pages/{name}/` | `{Name}Page.tsx` |
| sidebar | `src/pages/{context}/sidebars/` | `{Name}Sidebar.tsx` |
| form | `src/components/form/{name}/` | `Au{Name}.tsx` |
| svg | `src/components/svg/` | `{Name}Svg.tsx` |
| generic | `src/components/{name}/` | `{Name}.tsx` |

---

### 2. `/test:generate <component-path>`

**Purpose:** Generate tests for existing components

**Location:** `client/.claude/commands/test:generate.md`

**Features:**
- Analyzes component: PropsT, hooks, event handlers, conditionals
- Generates Vitest + Testing Library tests
- Follows existing test patterns in codebase
- Auto-mocks common dependencies
- Places in `__tests__/` next to component

**Test categories generated:**
- Rendering tests
- Props tests
- Interaction tests (onClick, onSubmit, etc.)
- Conditional rendering tests
- Hook behavior tests

---

### 3. `/visual:check [component-path] [--figma url] [--screenshot path]`

**Purpose:** Visual review comparing implementation to Figma designs

**Location:** `client/.claude/commands/visual:check.md`

**Features:**
- Captures screenshots via Playwright at mobile (375px) and desktop (1440px)
- User provides Figma link or screenshot as reference
- Claude compares images using vision capabilities
- Reports: color mismatches, spacing issues, responsive problems
- Validates CSS semantic variables usage

**Output:** Structured report with Critical/Minor findings and CSS suggestions

---

## Implementation Order

1. **`/component:create`** - Most straightforward, template-based
2. **`/test:generate`** - Requires component analysis logic
3. **`/visual:check`** - Most complex, needs Playwright + vision integration

## Files to Modify

**New files:**
- `client/.claude/commands/component:create.md`
- `client/.claude/commands/test:generate.md`
- `client/.claude/commands/visual:check.md`

**Reference files (read-only):**
- `client/.claude/commands/task:create.md` - Command pattern
- `client/.claude/docs/PATTERNS.md` - Component patterns
- `client/.claude/docs/COMPONENTS.md` - Naming conventions
- `client/src/components/sidebar/types/SidebarNameE.ts` - Sidebar enum
- `client/src/components/sidebar/types/SidebarPropsT.ts` - Sidebar props
- `client/playwright.config.ts` - Playwright setup

## Dependencies

- Playwright already installed (for visual:check)
- Vitest + Testing Library already configured (for test:generate)
- No new dependencies needed

## Review Time Reduction

| Tool | Reduces |
|------|---------|
| component:create | Boilerplate errors, naming mistakes, missing registrations |
| test:generate | Missing tests, inconsistent test structure |
| visual:check | UI discrepancies, hardcoded colors, responsive bugs |

# Agent Creator

Create a new custom agent following Claude Code best practices.

## Workflow

0. **Select scope** — global (~/.claude/) or project (.claude/)
1. **Gather requirements** — purpose, trigger, model
2. **Check existing agents** — avoid duplication
3. **Research patterns** — /docs agents, then web if needed
4. **Agent structure** — define with YAML frontmatter
5. **Create file** — write to selected scope
6. **Validate** — run agent-lint

## Step 0: Select Scope (MANDATORY)

Use `AskUserQuestion` to ask: Global (~/.claude/agents/) or Project (.claude/agents/)?

## Step 1: Gather Requirements (MANDATORY)

Ask using `AskUserQuestion`:

- **Purpose** — what task should this agent perform?
- **Trigger** — when should it be invoked?
- **Model** — haiku (fast), sonnet (balanced), opus (complex)

Only proceed when user confirms requirements.

## Step 2: Check Existing Agents

Check selected scope for similar agents. Also check other scope to avoid name conflicts.
If similar exists → propose extending instead of creating new.

## Step 3: Research Patterns (MANDATORY)

1. Use SlashCommand tool to invoke `/docs agents` for Claude Code patterns
2. Use WebSearch for community examples and current best practices (MANDATORY)

Output before continuing:

- Key patterns found
- Pitfalls to avoid

## Step 4: Agent Structure

### Writing for Claude (not humans)

Claude doesn't need:
- Full JSON examples for built-in tools — just tool name + key params
- Multiple examples of same pattern — one clear example enough
- External links — not accessible at runtime
- Decorative formatting — `---` dividers, excessive whitespace
- "Remember:" summaries — Claude retains context

Claude benefits from:
- Explicit constraints and boundaries
- One good/bad example pair per concept
- Tables for mappings (compact, scannable)
- Bullet points over prose

```markdown
---
name: agent-name
description: When to invoke (Claude uses this to decide)
tools: Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep, Bash
model: sonnet
color: blue
---

# Agent Title

One-sentence role.

## Input Sources

- `path/to/input` — description

## Core Responsibilities

### 1. First Task

Instructions with examples.

## Output Format

Save to: `path/to/output.md`
First line: `Status: Done | Failed - reason | Needs Review - explanation`

## Rules

- DO: specific behaviors
- DON'T: anti-patterns
```

### Right Altitude

| Too Rigid                            | Optimal                          | Too Vague            |
| ------------------------------------ | -------------------------------- | -------------------- |
| "Step 1: Open X, Step 2: Find Y..." | Clear goals + flexible execution | "Do the thing well" |

### YAML Fields

| Field       | Required | Notes                               |
| ----------- | -------- | ----------------------------------- |
| name        | Yes      | lowercase, hyphens                  |
| description | Yes      | Claude uses for invocation decision |
| tools       | No       | Whitelist; omit to inherit all      |
| model       | No       | opus/sonnet/haiku/inherit           |

### Model Selection

| Model  | Use When                                |
| ------ | --------------------------------------- |
| haiku  | Fast, simple tasks (2x speed, 3x cheaper) |
| sonnet | Default, balanced                       |
| opus   | Complex reasoning, architecture         |

### Prompting Best Practices Checklist

Before creating, verify:

- [ ] **Complete world picture** — agent knows environment and capabilities
- [ ] **Consistent instructions** — no contradictions
- [ ] **No surprises** — inputs/outputs documented
- [ ] **User perspective** — focus on what user cares about
- [ ] **Varied examples** — not just happy path
- [ ] **Critical rules prominent** — important rules at beginning

### Thinking Mode Integration

For agents needing multi-step reasoning, include in prompts:
- "think step by step" — basic reasoning
- "think harder" — complex analysis
- "ultrathink" — deep architectural decisions

Budget ~2-3x tokens for thinking overhead.

## Step 5: Create File

### Before Writing

Ask yourself:
1. Would Claude behave differently without this section? If no → remove
2. Is this the same info in different words? → deduplicate
3. Is this obvious to an LLM? (JSON syntax, markdown format) → skip
4. Can this table be 3 rows instead of 10? → condense

Location: `{scope}/agents/{agent-name}.md`

Verify:

1. Valid YAML frontmatter
2. Clear description with trigger
3. Output format documented
4. Rules include DO and DON'T

## Step 6: Validate with Agent Lint

Run validation before finalizing using SlashCommand:

```bash
/agent:lint {path} --r ultrathink
```

### Validation Checklist

| Check | Action if Failed |
|-------|------------------|
| Description vague | Make trigger-specific |
| Tool overreach | Remove unnecessary tools |
| Conflict detected | Rename or merge with existing |
| Missing examples | Add good/bad examples |

### Handling Validation Results

- **PASS** → Report success to user
- **WARN** → Show warnings, ask if user wants to address
- **FAIL** → Must fix before completing

Do NOT skip validation. It catches issues before they cause problems.

Apply recommendations that align with user's intent. Ask about scope expansions.

## Output

```
## Agent Created

**File:** {path}
**Purpose:** [brief]
**Trigger:** [when invoked]
**Model:** [selected]

Test by: [suggestion]
```

## Rules

- Check existing agents first
- Use sonnet as default model
- Keep descriptions specific (not "helps with code")
- Include DO and DON'T examples
- Document output format
- Never give all tools unless needed

## Anti-Patterns

| Anti-Pattern           | Why It's Bad                  | Better Approach                 |
| ---------------------- | ----------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Hardcoded step-by-step | Brittle, breaks on variations | Describe goals, let agent decide |
| No examples            | Agent guesses behavior        | Include good/bad examples       |
| Vague description      | Claude doesn't know when      | Specific trigger conditions     |
| Too many tools         | Security risk, confusion      | Whitelist only needed tools     |
| No output format       | Inconsistent results          | Document exact format           |

## Example: Code Reviewer Agent

```markdown
---
name: code-reviewer
description: Reviews code changes for bugs, security issues, and best practices. Use after developer completes implementation.
tools: Read, Glob, Grep
model: sonnet
---

# Code Reviewer

Review code changes and provide actionable feedback.

## Input

- File paths or git diff

## Review Checklist

### Security
- [ ] No hardcoded secrets
- [ ] Input validation present
- [ ] No SQL injection risks

### Quality
- [ ] Functions under 50 lines
- [ ] Clear naming
- [ ] Error handling present

## Output Format

## Code Review: {file}

**Status:** Approved | Changes Requested

### Issues Found
1. **[Severity]** Description
   - Location: file:line
   - Suggestion: how to fix

### Positive Notes
- What's done well

## Rules

- DO: Focus on bugs and security first
- DO: Provide specific line references
- DON'T: Nitpick style if linter handles it
- DON'T: Rewrite code, just suggest
```
